title: How to Write Prompts That Scale Across Your Team
description: Consistency matters more than clever wording. Here is a simple framework we use to document prompts that anyone can pick up.
content:
When a prompt works for one person but fails for everyone else, the problem is rarely the model. It is usually missing context, unclear constraints, or inconsistent structure.

We use a four-part template for every shared prompt: role, context, task, and output format. The role sets expertise level. Context covers audience, product, and constraints. The task states exactly what to produce. Output format defines length, tone, and structure.

Start by auditing prompts that already work. Copy the structure, not the wording. Replace specifics with bracketed placeholders like [product name] so teammates can adapt quickly.

Version prompts when you change them. A simple changelog at the top — date, author, what changed — prevents confusion when results shift.

Finally, test with someone outside your team. If they get usable output on the first try without asking questions, the prompt is ready to publish.

title: Five Prompt Patterns for Better Marketing Copy
description: From audience framing to constraint-based drafts, these patterns help you move from blank page to usable copy faster.
content:
Marketing copy prompts fail when they ask for everything at once. These five patterns break the work into reliable steps.

Audience framing: describe who reads this, what they want, and what they fear. Constraint-based drafts: set word limits, banned words, and required proof points before generating.

Variation sets: ask for three versions with different angles — outcome-focused, problem-focused, and social proof — then combine the best lines.

Edit passes: run a second prompt that only tightens and removes fluff without changing meaning.

Channel adaptation: take one core message and reshape it for email subject lines, ad headlines, and landing page heroes.

Keep a swipe file of prompts that produced copy you actually shipped. That library becomes your team's competitive advantage.

title: Building a Shared Prompt Library for Startups
description: What to include, how to organize categories, and when to retire prompts that no longer match your product.
content:
A prompt library is not a dump of everything you have tried. It is a curated set of templates tied to real workflows.

Organize by job-to-be-done, not by tool. Categories like marketing, writing, and research map to how people search when they are under deadline.

Each entry needs a title, short description, the full prompt, tags, and an example output. Without the example, new hires cannot tell good from mediocre.

Assign owners. Every prompt should have someone responsible for keeping it current. Review quarterly or after major product changes.

Retire aggressively. Outdated prompts erode trust faster than a small library builds it. Archive with a note explaining why — that context saves future debate.

Measure usage, not vanity counts. Track which prompts get copied and which get ignored. Double down on what ships work.

title: Why Placeholder Variables Make Prompts Reusable
description: Bracketed placeholders turn one-off prompts into templates your whole team can adapt in seconds.
content:
Hard-coded details make prompts brittle. The moment a product name or audience changes, the prompt needs a full rewrite.

Placeholders like [audience], [tone], and [word limit] separate structure from specifics. They signal what to customize and keep the skeleton intact.

Name placeholders clearly. [Product] is ambiguous if you run multiple lines. [Product name — consumer app] removes guesswork.

Provide defaults in comments when helpful: [tone: professional and direct]. New users get guidance without cluttering the main prompt.

Document required vs optional placeholders at the top. Required fields prevent incomplete runs. Optional fields keep power users efficient.

This small habit is the difference between a personal note and infrastructure your company can rely on.
